content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 197–198 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_002_page_001.jpg, DEU_002_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 144–145

---

1 "Then we turned and set out into the wilderness by the way of the Red Sea, as the LORD spoke to me, and we circled around Mount Seir many days.

2 And the LORD said to me:

3 You have been circling around this mountain long enough. Turn northward.

4 And command the people, saying: You are about to pass through the territory of your brothers, the children of Esau, who dwell in Seir; and they will be afraid of you. So be very careful.

5 Do not contend with them, for I will not give you any of their land, not so much as a footstep, because I have given Mount Seir to Esau as a possession.

6 You shall purchase food from them with silver so that you may eat, and you shall also buy water from them with silver so that you may drink.

7 For the LORD your God has blessed you in all the work of your hands. He knows your wandering through this great wilderness. These forty years the LORD your God has been with you; you have lacked nothing.

8 So we passed by from our brothers, the children of Esau who dwell in Seir, away from the Arabah road, away from Elath and from Ezion-geber. And we turned and passed by the way of the wilderness of Moab.

9 And the LORD said to me: Do not harass Moab, and do not contend with them in battle, for I will not give you any of their land as a possession, because I have given Ar to the children of Lot as a possession.

10 The Emim formerly dwelt there — a people great and numerous and tall as the Anakim.

11 They too were regarded as Rephaim, like the Anakim, but the Moabites called them Emim.

12 In Seir the Horites formerly dwelt, but the children of Esau dispossessed them and destroyed them from before them and settled in their place, just as Israel did to the land of his possession, which the LORD gave to them.

13 Now rise up and cross over the Wadi Zered. So we crossed over the Wadi Zered.

14 And the days we traveled from Kadesh-barnea until we crossed the Wadi Zered were thirty-eight years, until all the generation of the men of war had perished from the midst of the camp, as the LORD had sworn to them.

15 Moreover, the hand of the LORD was against them, to confound them from the midst of the camp, until they had perished.

16 So it came about when all the men of war had finally perished from among the people,

17 that the LORD spoke to me, saying:

18 Today you are to cross the border of Moab at Ar.

19 And when you approach opposite the children of Ammon, do not harass them or contend with them, for I will not give you any of the land of the children of Ammon as a possession, because I have given it to the children of Lot as a possession.

20 That too is regarded as the land of the Rephaim. Rephaim formerly dwelt there, but the Ammonites called them Zamzummim —

21 a people great and numerous and tall as the Anakim; but the LORD destroyed them before them, and they dispossessed them and settled in their place,

22 just as he did for the children of Esau who dwell in Seir, when he destroyed the Horites before them — they dispossessed them and settled in their place to this day.

23 And the Avvim, who dwelt in villages as far as Gaza — the Caphtorim who came from Caphtor, destroyed them and settled in their place.

24 Rise up, set out, and cross the Wadi Arnon. See, I have given into your hand Sihon the Amorite king of Heshbon and his land. Begin to take possession, and contend with him in battle.

25 This day I will begin to put the dread of you and the fear of you before the peoples who are under the whole heaven, who shall hear the report of you and tremble and writhe because of you.

26 So I sent messengers from the wilderness of Kedemoth to Sihon king of Heshbon with words of peace, saying:

27 Let me pass through your land; I will go only along the road; I will turn neither to the right nor to the left.

28 Food for silver you shall sell me so that I may eat, and water for silver you shall give me so that I may drink. Only let me pass through on foot —

29 as the children of Esau who dwell in Seir did for me, and the Moabites who dwell in Ar — until I cross the Jordan into the land that the LORD our God is giving to us.

30 But Sihon king of Heshbon was not willing to let us pass through, because the LORD your God hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate, so that he might give him into your hand, as at this day.

31 And the LORD said to me: See, I have begun to give Sihon and his land before you. Begin to take possession, to inherit his land.

32 And Sihon came out against us — he and all his people — to battle at Jahaz.

33 And the LORD our God gave him before us, and we struck him down, and his sons and all his people.

34 And we captured all his cities at that time and devoted them to destruction — men, women, and children; we left no survivor.

35 Only the livestock we plundered for ourselves, along with the spoil of the cities that we captured.

36 From Aroer, which is on the edge of the Wadi Arnon, and from the city that is in the wadi, as far as Gilead, there was not a city too high for us. The LORD our God gave them all before us.

37 Only to the land of the children of Ammon you did not draw near — all the banks of the Wadi Jabbok, and the cities of the hill country, and whatever the LORD our God had forbidden to us.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**"Children of Esau" / "children of Lot" / "children of Ammon":** LOGOS consistently renders bene + proper noun as "children of X" throughout this chapter. The LXX uses huioi uniformly. This preserves the genealogical consciousness that structures the entire passage — Edom (Esau), Moab and Ammon (Lot) are kin, not enemies, and God has already given them their portions.

**"Not so much as a footstep" (v.5):** The Hebrew is ke-midras kaf-ragel — "as much as the sole of a foot treads." LOGOS compresses this to "a footstep" rather than a more expanded paraphrase, preserving the idiomatic force. The LXX: oude bēma podos.

**Pre-Israelite peoples — Emim, Rephaim, Horites, Avvim, Caphtorim, Zamzummim (vv.10–23):** LOGOS retains all ethnonyms untranslated. This parenthetical section (a kind of antiquarian digression) is one of the literary peculiarities of Deuteronomy 2. The LXX preserves them as well, though with some spelling variation (Enakim, Ommaein for Emim, Zomzommin for Zamzummim).

**"Devoted them to destruction" (v.34, nakharem):** The Hebrew root kharam is the technical term for sacred destruction of enemy peoples and their property in holy war — the cherem. LOGOS uses "devoted to destruction" as the established formal-equivalence rendering, preserving the theological valence while remaining comprehensible. The LXX: exōlethreusamen, "utterly destroyed."

**"Hardened his spirit and made his heart obstinate" (v.30):** The Hebrew has two distinct terms: hiqshah (hardened, made rigid) and imets (strengthened, made firm/obstinate). LOGOS preserves both in parallel. The LXX: eskleryne kyrios ho theos hēmōn to pneuma autou, using only one verb but preserving "spirit."

**"Words of peace" (v.26, divrei shalom):** A diplomatic formula. LOGOS preserves "words of peace" rather than "a peaceful message" — the phrase is formulaic and should not be normalized. The LXX: rhēmata eirēnika.

**Verse 37 — Ammon excepted:** The chapter closes with a limiting clause: Israel obeyed the divine boundary regarding Ammon. This is structurally and theologically significant — the narrative establishes both the scope and the limits of the conquest, and LOGOS renders the verse exactly as it stands.

*Translation by LOGOS | Deuteronomy Chapter 2 | LOGOS Bible (Formal Equivalence)*
